Global Change Biology




   RESEARCH ARTICLE                    OPEN ACCESS




Mapping Resilient Landscapes to Climate Change in
a Megadiverse Country
Milena Fermina Rosenfield1          | Lucas Jardim2      | Marina Antongiovanni3             | Luciano Carramaschi de Alagão Querido4,5                           |
Alisson André Ribeiro  6      | Andrea Sánchez-Tapia   7      | Priscila Silveira 7,8     | Levi Carina Terribile2    |

Keywords: biodiversity | climate adaptation | climate resilience | connectivity | conservation | geodiversity | restoration


   ABSTRACT
  The effects of global climate change on biodiversity and ecosystem functioning are unevenly distributed in the geographic space.
  Identifying sites more suitable to sustain biodiversity in a changing climate is essential to both species conservation and restoration
  strategies at different scales. Here, we map terrestrial climate-­resilient sites for biodiversity across Brazil to identify sites with greater
  chances of providing suitable conditions for species to persist under regional climate change. Our mapping combines spatial metrics
  based on landscape heterogeneity, a proxy for microclimatic variability, and local connectedness, a measure of connectivity between
  habitats, to determine landscape resilience, assuming that resilience to climate change will be greater the more heterogeneous the
  characteristics of local habitats are and the more connected they are in the landscape. Our results show that within each biome,
  medium to high resilient sites are mostly found in the Amazon (40% of the biome) and Pantanal (38%). Low resilience, conversely,
  is concentrated in the Atlantic Forest (41% of the biome), followed by Cerrado (37%), Pampa (36%), and Caatinga (34%). Landscape
  resilience information has the potential to be used to effectively guide decision-­making and public policy on strategies for conserva-
  tion, restoration, and sustainable use practices. Priority for conservation should be on high resilience sites as they have the potential
  to sustain biodiversity in face of undergoing and future climate change. Other approaches could be used in situations of medium to
  low resilience also, such as: conservation of current corridors in sites with high local connectedness, but low landscape heterogeneity;


For affiliations refer to page 14.

This is an open access article under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution License, which permits use, distribution and reproduction in any medium,
provided the original work is properly cited.
© 2025 The Author(s). Global Change Biology published by John Wiley & Sons Ltd.


Global Change Biology, 2025; 31:e70544                                                                                                                        1 of 17
https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.70544
                                                                                                                                                  13652486, 2025, 10, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/gcb.70544 by Capes, Wiley Online Library on [10/10/2025]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License
  restoration of natural vegetation on sites that show high landscape heterogeneity, but low local connectedness; and sustainable prac-
  tices in areas of low resilience. Our study provides an updated method to pinpoint climate-­resilient sites for biodiversity which was
  applied to a megadiverse country but is applicable to any ecosystem around the globe.


1   |   Introduction                                                      According to the degree of exposure and a set of biological

tributes (Bender et al. 2019). Beyond the impact of climate change,       sphere and topography results in topoclimates, which can

factors (Manes et al. 2021).                                              particularly for traditional and indigenous communities cop-

2 of 17                                                                                                             Global Change Biology, 2025
                                                                                                                                             13652486, 2025, 10, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/gcb.70544 by Capes, Wiley Online Library on [10/10/2025].   BOX 1    |    Glossary of terms used in this study.

    Term                                                                          Meaning used in this study
    Landforms                                                         Topographical features present in the landscape, such
                                                                     as mountain tops, valleys, and gorges. Each topographic
                                                                   feature suffers different levels of exposure to solar radiation,
                                                                       wind, and humidity, and the variability of landforms
                                                                        is used as a proxy for environmental heterogeneity
                                                                           and the diversity of landscape's microclimates.
    Landscape                                                  A spatially delimited area with heterogeneity in different aspects
                                                                 (landforms, elevation, resistance, and spatial configuration)
                                                                   capable of influencing the ecological processes within it.
    Landscape heterogeneity                                      An estimate of the variability of habitats and microclimates,
                                                                  defined by landforms, elevation range, wetland score, and
                                                               richness of soils present in the surroundings of a given location.
    Landscape resilience                                              The capacity of an area to absorb the impacts of climate
                                                                      change on species diversity and ecological functions. A
                                                                     resilient landscape presents variability of conditions that
                                                                   support biodiversity, maintaining fundamental relationships
                                                                     between ecological components, and allows for adaptive
                                                                     change in species composition and ecosystem structure.
    Landscape resistance                                                  Difficulty imposed by land use and land cover
                                                                             classes on the movement of organisms.
    Local connectedness                                            A measure of how easy it is for organisms to move between
                                                                    landscape elements (or types of land use and land cover).
                                                                    Local connectedness is influenced both by the resistance
                                                                      of landscape elements to the movement of organisms
                                                                        and by the spatial arrangement of these elements.


While not capturing exposure or species-­specific sensitivi-         Brazil stands out not only for its exceptional biodiversity but
ties directly, landscape resilience can serve as a foundational      also for its vast territorial, climatic, and socio-­ecological diver-
layer to be integrated with vulnerability and risk assessments       sity, making it a strategic country for investigating landscape
(Foden et al. 2019), ultimately supporting more robust, mul-         resilience to climate change. Home to over 203 million peo-
tiscale strategies for adaptation and conservation. Landscape        ple (IBGE 2023) and six major terrestrial biomes—Amazon,
resilience is expected to be higher where there are a variety        Atlantic Forest, Caatinga, Cerrado, Pampa, and Pantanal—
of microclimate conditions that can be accessed by different         Brazil hosts a wide range of ecosystems, from equatorial rain-
species and also locally adapted populations. Landscapes with        forests to semiarid shrublands. These regions are subject to
lower structural connectivity reduce the ability of species to       differentiated impacts of climate change, such as prolonged
track suitable climatic conditions, increasing the deleterious       droughts in the Amazon (Marengo et al. 2018), rising tempera-
effects of climatic change, an effect that is stronger for poor      tures in the Cerrado (Lapola et al. 2011), and increase in inten-
dispersers. Besides the biological requirements needed for           sity and frequency of surface air temperature extremes along the
tracking suitable conditions, species need a spatial network         western South Atlantic coast (Sanches et al. 2023). Projections
of sites that are not only climatically suitable but also pro-       indicate that the country could face temperature increases of up
vide low resistance to movement (Borges and Loyola 2020;             to 3°C–6°C, depending on the biome, by the end of the century
Mantyka-­P ringle et al. 2012; Tourinho et al. 2022). Moreover,      (MCTI 2013), which could dramatically affect ecosystems, agri-
in the context of socio-­ecological systems, traditional and in-     cultural productivity, and water availability. Additionally, Brazil
digenous communities used to move across large, heteroge-            faces intense anthropogenic pressures, including deforestation,
neous landscapes, utilizing microclimatic refuges for shelter,       habitat fragmentation, and unsustainable land use practices,
resource management, and other activities. However, today            which exacerbate biodiversity loss (Barlow et al. 2016; de Lima
most of them reside in limited areas due to fragmentation or         et al. 2020). These combined challenges reinforce the urgency
demarcation of ethnic territories. Hence, adequate conserva-         of identifying resilient areas and developing adaptive strategies
tion strategies and climate change mitigation and adaptation         tailored to the regional ecological and social context.
strategies depend therefore on integrative approaches that
consider these multiple and synergistic effects of global and        Recent theoretical and operational advances, such as the greater

2   |   Methods                                                             tribute to landscape resilience (Anderson et al. 2023; Zhang
                                                                            et al. 2016), even if they are impacted by climate change
We identified climate resilient sites by combining data on                  in the future. We used data from the Global Wetlands
landscape heterogeneity and local connectedness (Rosenfield                 Database (Gumbricht et al. 2017), resampled from 231 m to
et al. 2024). We assessed resilience across Brazil, aiming to cap-          a 90 m resolution. The wetland score is a combination of
ture the different regions and types of vegetation grouped in               wetland densities at local and regional scales, and also wet-
Brazilian biomes (IBGE 2021c): Amazônia (the rainforests in                 land patchiness at a regional scale, defined as the number
north Brazil), Caatinga (the seasonally dry forests in the north-           of wetlands in the area. These variables capture the dis-
east), Cerrado (the savannas in central Brazil), Mata Atlântica             tribution of humid areas within the landscape, which are
(the seasonal forests and rainforests along the coast, from south           essential for maintaining biological diversity and provid-
to northeast), Pampa (the grasslands in the south), and Pantanal            ing ecological services, such as decomposition, biochemi-
(the wetlands in the central-­west) (Appendix S1: Figure S1).               cal transformation, storage, and production of living plants
We only evaluated terrestrial environments. Although rivers,                and animals (Ryeland et al. 2021; Zedler and Kercher 2005).
lakes, and wetlands were included in the construction of the                The wetland score was obtained by averaging the density of
landscape heterogeneity and local connectedness layers (as de-              wetlands within circular areas of 450 m radius (local scale)
scribed below), water bodies, and other aquatic ecosystems were             and 1170 m radius (regional scale) around each 90 m cell.
masked from the final resilience map. They were, therefore, not             In sites where wetland patchiness (within a circular area of
classified as any class of landscape resilience. A general over-            1170 m radius) was higher than the previous wetland score,
view of the analysis is provided below, and the full description,           we combined values as the weighted average of local den-
as well as links to the tutorials for running the analysis, are pre-        sity, regional density, and regional patchiness, weighting
sented in Appendix S1 of this manuscript.                                   local density twice as the other variables.

  d. Soil richness: in places with low variation in landform          Based on a land use land cover map of Brazil, we assigned resis-
     variety, elevation range, and wetland score, soil richness       tance values to each class listed in the database, considering their
     becomes an important component of environmental het-             degree of human intervention and resistance to the movement of
     erogeneity in the landscape that could support biodiversity.     organisms. We assigned the lowest values (least resistance) to
     Soil richness is the number of dominant and subdom-              natural areas and the highest values (greatest resistance) to in-
     inant soil types from the pedology database 1:250.000            tensely human-­impacted areas (urban areas), with a gradient of
     of the Brazilian Institute of Geography and Statistics           values between the two extremes (Appendix S1: Table S1). We ob-
     (IBGE 2021b), rasterized to a 90 m resolution image.             tained land use land cover data from MapBiomas Project (2023)
                                                                      and complemented it with layers of infrastructure of transport
The layer of landscape heterogeneity is generated by combin-          and energy (ANEEL 2023; IBGE 2021a). Depending on their
ing the four variables described above after data standardiza-        characteristics, rivers can represent barriers to the movement
tion (Z-­score calculation). Landform variety, elevation range,       of species across the landscape (Coelho et al. 2022; Hayes and
wetland score, and soil richness were converted to Z-­scores,         Sewlal 2004), so we assigned different resistance weights to riv-
assuming means and standard deviations within moving                  ers and lakes depending on their width.
windows with 200 pixels radius. This ensures the inclusion
of distinct physical and environmental conditions, and allows         Once resistance values had been assigned, we estimated local con-
local variations in the composition of the associated biota to        nectedness as the inverse of the weighted average of the resistances
be captured, in addition to providing a smooth transition be-         within a circular moving window of 23 pixels (approximately
tween biomes. This alternative was applied so that it would           2070 m), which represents the spatial context in which each pixel is
not be necessary to standardize within the biome and avoid            inserted. To calculate the weighted average, we used a linear decay
having problems in transitional areas. The combination pro-           function (kernel filter), which recognizes that closer pixels have a
cedure follows a hierarchical evaluation that considers the ad-       greater influence on each other than more distant pixels, influenc-
ditional effect of the following variable in the previous one.        ing the local connectedness value of each pixel. The kernel filter
The evaluation starts with the z-­scores of landform variety;         was applied to the resistance layer and reflects not only the land-
then, for sites with higher elevation range, landscape hetero-        scape composition but is also influenced by the landscape config-
geneity receives the weighted average of both variables, giv-         uration (i.e., the spatial context) surrounding each pixel. This step
ing double the weight to landform variety; then the same is           was conducted to find paths that offer the least potential resistance
done for wetland score and finally for soil richness, comparing       to species movement, based on the local context of the landscape.
every combination of the four variables. For the general cal-
culation of landscape heterogeneity, the weight given to land-
form variety was higher than any other variable. Only in flat         2.3   |   Landscape Resilience
areas, where landform variability is lower, the variable wet-

this manuscript.                                                      of both variables. To facilitate interpretation, the classes generated

2.2   |   Local Connectedness                                         24; quadrant Q3—classes 31, 32, 41, 42; and quadrant Q4—classes
                                                                      33, 34, 43, 44. These quadrants indicate both lower (Q1) and higher
Local connectedness is a measure of how much resistance land-         (Q4) resilience, and the greater effect of the landscape heteroge-
scape elements (or types of land use and land cover) offer to the     neity (Q2) or local connectedness (Q3) layers. From these classes,
movement of species. We used the structural differences of the        we generated a bivariate map of landscape resilience, which aims
various types of land use and land cover as proxies of resistance,    to show the areas where the results of the two metrics converge or
with natural areas being less resistant, and highly impacted areas    diverge, identifying areas where landscape heterogeneity and local
being more resistant to the movement of terrestrial organisms in      connectedness are more important for landscape resilience.
the landscape. Connectedness is greater where landscape offers
less resistance to the movement of terrestrial organisms, repre-
senting the degree of landscape permeability (or the inverse of       2.4   |   Data Analysis
the degree of resistance) around each cell evaluated. The current
approach did not take into account differences in the dispersal ca-   After generating the data for each layer, we conducted further
pacity of organisms, but relies on the basic assumption that well-­   analysis to explore the results regionally, considering the vari-
preserved native habitats with similar characteristics in close       ations across Brazil and within biomes. For landscape hetero-
connection to one another represent better passages and refuges       geneity, we assessed the contribution of each component (i.e.,
for biota than landscapes where patches of well-­preserved hab-       landform variety, elevation range, wetland score, and soil rich-
itat are isolated by matrices of degraded or contrasting habitats     ness) to the final value of this layer across Brazil. And for land-
and distant from one another.                                         scape resilience, we evaluated the percentage distribution of

the distribution of pixels in the original images of landscape heterogeneity and local connectedness.

3.1   |   Landscape Heterogeneity                                          portions. The northwest of the Amazônia biome is the portion
                                                                           with the highest concentration of natural areas, with lower re-
We found that areas of high landscape heterogeneity in Brazil              sistance values and therefore high connectedness. The Pantanal
are disjointly distributed (Figure 2), highlighting locations              also appears as a region with higher local connectedness.
with rough terrain, abrupt changes in slope, high density of

acteristics, the components that make up landscape heteroge-               3.3   |   Landscape Resilience
neity (i.e., landform variety, elevation range, wetland score,
and soil richness) had different contributions depending on                Our results provide information on the most suitable areas asso-
the local context. Overall, landform variety was the main vari-            ciated with climate resilience, considering the variability of habi-
able in determining landscape heterogeneity, followed by the               tats, and local connectedness between natural vegetation patches.

3.2   |   Local Connectedness                                              in the southeastern coastal area of Mata Atlântica, and in the
                                                                           regions with the roughest terrain or shallow soils in the Pampa
The local connectedness layer has a different pattern to that pre-         (Figure 4). The spatial pattern of resilience is guided by a more
sented in the landscape heterogeneity layer, showing a division            regional pattern of local connectedness, refined by a more local
between highly connected regions in the north and others with              variation in landscape heterogeneity. For example, regions with
lower connectedness in the central and southeastern regions of             low anthropogenic impact, such as most regions in Amazônia,
the country (Figure 3). This distribution indicates a clear frag-          vary their resilience levels according to the local distribution of
mentation of the Brazilian map into two portions. This is due              the diversity of the physical environment. Conversely, despite the
to the historical occupation of these regions and their current            high heterogeneity of the landscape across Mata Atlântica, its
use in terms of agriculture and urban occupation. The pattern              low connectedness reduces the areas of greatest resilience.

scores of the variables. Landscape heterogeneity takes into consideration: Landform variety, elevation range, wetland score, and soil richness. Higher
and lower values for landscape heterogeneity are shown in a gradient from red to blue. Biome names: Amazônia (Amazon; rainforests), Caatinga (dry
forests), Cerrado (savannas), Mata Atlântica (Atlantic forest; seasonal forests and rainforests), Pampa (grasslands), and Pantanal (wetlands). Map
lines delineate study areas and do not necessarily depict accepted national boundaries.

FIGURE 4    |    Landscape resilience resulting from the combination of landscape heterogeneity and local connectedness maps, classified by quar-
tiles. The bivariate classification of the landscape resilience layer was divided into four quadrants (Q1, Q2, Q3, and Q4) with different degrees
of local connectedness and landscape heterogeneity. White portions in the map, more visible in the biome boxes, represent water bodies. Biome
names: Amazônia (Amazon; rainforests), Caatinga (dry forests), Cerrado (savannas), Mata Atlântica (Atlantic forest; seasonal forests and rainfor-
ests), Pampa (grasslands), and Pantanal (wetlands). Map lines delineate study areas and do not necessarily depict accepted national boundaries.



TABLE 1    |    Percentages for each quadrant across Brazil and within biomes. Results are relative to the area calculated for Brazil (Across Brazil),
and relative to the area of the biome (Within biome).

 Quadrant               Amazônia (%)             Caatinga (%)           Cerrado (%)           Mata Atlântica (%)              Pampa (%)           Pantanal (%)
 Across Brazil
 Quadrant Q1                    5.5                     3.4                   8.8                        5.6                        0.9                  0.2
 Quadrant Q2                    6.0                     2.6                    7.7                       6.6                        0.9                  0.1
 Quadrant Q3                   17.3                     2.0                    3.3                       0.6                        0.2                  0.7
 Quadrant Q4                   19.5                     2.1                   4.0                        0.8                        0.4                  0.7
 Total                         48.3                    10.1                   23.8                       13.7                      2.4                   1.8
 Within biome
 Quadrant Q1                   11.4                    34.0                   37.1                       41.1                      36.0                 13.8

Pampa (grasslands), and Pantanal (wetlands).

(D), Pampa (E), and Pantanal (F). Values shown in circles are the percentage of each class relative to the area of the biome. Larger and darker circles
indicate higher percentages. Biome names: Amazônia (Amazon; rainforests), Caatinga (dry forests), Cerrado (savannas), Mata Atlântica (Atlantic
forest; seasonal forests and rainforests), Pampa (grasslands), and Pantanal (wetlands).

the Araguaia National Park, in the state of Mato Grosso). (a) Details highlighting the four quadrants of estimated resilience based on the bivariate
color scale are shown in figures (b–e). The order of the maps follows the same representation shown for the quadrants in the landscape resilience
diagram: (b) areas classified in quadrant Q2, with high landscape heterogeneity but low local connectedness; (c) the most resilient areas, classi-
fied in quadrant Q4, with high landscape heterogeneity and high local connectedness; (d) the least resilient areas, classified in quadrant Q1, with
low landscape heterogeneity and low local connectedness; and (e) areas classified in quadrant Q3, with low landscape heterogeneity but high local
connectedness.


connectedness in places that, as indicated in the landscape resil-          biological flow and consequently promotes the restoration of

classes from low to high local connectedness (from left to right):          to class 24 (the same example applies to class 24 to 34, and 34
for example, directing actions at places indicated as class 14 so           to 44), it means that the outcomes of the restoration activities
that, in the future, local connectedness increases and helps to             are maximized, potentially increasing vegetation structure and
improve the landscape by connecting these sites, which aids                 ecosystem functioning.

As mentioned before, our database can be used to identify areas
for restoration as resilience to climate change is a relevant crite-

et al. 2010). We can prioritize different restoration strategies,      Milena Fermina Rosenfield: conceptualization, investigation,

Governments of megadiversity countries have the responsibil-           ology, validation, writing – review and editing. Fernando Gertum

ity of managing their territories, by conserving or improving

patible with biodiversity and ecosystem functioning. Climate           ing – review and editing. Cintia Cornelius: methodology, validation,
change will likely make both tasks more difficult, since species       writing – review and editing. Geraldo Alves Damasceno-­Junior:
and human resources are expected to change their distribution          methodology, validation, writing – review and editing. Ricardo

stakeholders. Landscape resilience information can be used

for evaluating the existing network of protected areas and its         validation, writing – review and editing. Marcia C. M. Marques:

tion from this study. This map could contribute to the National        Ribeiro de Morais: methodology, validation, writing – review and

currently under revision by the Brazilian government, and as           – review and editing. Andreza Viana Neri: methodology, validation,
